summary: The extension has several real correctness and security problems: exported `map.html` edit mode is still wired to the legacy localhost:8088 `/edit` path and JSON response shape, while the current canvas-engine edit flow is a different service/protocol; the pull pipeline fetches assets with a plain background `fetch()` that cannot use web-audit provider routes like `--via brightdata` or `--stealth` and does not request credentials, so protected pages will break; the happy-path `buildAnatomyV1()` shape matches the current draft-07/Zod contract, but the import path does not validate incoming `anx

issues.description: `map.html` edit mode is still hard-wired to `fetch(DAEMON + '/edit')` on the legacy localhost:8088 daemon and expects a one-shot JSON payload with `elapsedMs`, `bytesBefore`, and `bytesAfter`. That flow is stale: the extension comments still describe the removed Claude `/edit` rewrite path, and the current canvas-engine server exposes a different edit service/protocol on a different port (`POST /e
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the dead map-edit button until there is a supported backend, or rewire it to the current canvas-engine edit API and consume its actual response format instead of assuming a JSON 200 from `localhost:8088/edit`.

issues.description: The pull pipeline proxies every asset through `chrome.runtime.sendMessage({ type: 'anatomy:fetch', url })`, and the service worker handles that with a bare `fetch(url, { redirect:'follow', credentials:'omit' })`. There is no way to route requests through web-audit's provider modes such as `--via brightdata` or `--stealth`, and even ordinary authenticated assets lose cookies because the content s
issues.suggested_fix: Extend the fetch message contract to carry fetch strategy and auth intent, then route protected pulls through the same provider/browser machinery that web-audit uses for WAF-protected pages. At minimum, plumb `credentials:'include'` where the page origin requires cookies and separate top-level HTML capture from asset fetching.

issues.description: On the native labeling path, `buildAnatomyV1()` emits a contract-compatible envelope. The problem is the import path: `doImport()` accepts any object with `version === 'anatomy-v1'` and an array of `regions`, copies fields through without validating them, and `normalize()` only fills a missing `id`/`layer`/`note`/`fetches`. An imported region with a missing `name` becomes `label: undefined`, and `
issues.suggested_fix: Validate imports with the same `AnatomyV1` schema/Zod validator before merging them into extension state, or at least coerce and reject invalid `name`, `layer`, `fetches`, and `bounds` fields before they can be re-exported.

issues.description: Several `innerHTML`/template-string render paths interpolate `layer` without escaping it. That value is user-controlled through imported JSON (`doImport()` copies `r.layer` straight into state), then reused in the HUD (`${layer}`) and exported map HTML (`data-layer="${r.layer}"`). A malicious import can therefore inject markup into the content-script UI and into the downloaded `ma
issues.suggested_fix: Treat imported `layer` values as untrusted: validate them against the canonical enum before storing them, and escape any remaining dynamic attribute/text insertions instead of interpolating raw strings into `innerHTML`.

issues.description: Version provenance has already drifted. `manifest.json` declares `0.3.15`, pull metadata uses `anatomy-extension@0.3.15`, but the exported anatomy envelope still stamps `tools: ['anatomy-extension@0.3.11']`. That means two outputs produced by the same build report different producer versions, which undermines debugging and schema/provenance audits.
issues.suggested_fix: Define one extension version constant and reuse it for the manifest-adjacent runtime metadata, export envelopes, and pull envelopes so the strings cannot diverge.

issues.description: The extension is over-permissioned for MV3. It requests `host_permissions: ["<all_urls>"]` and injects both content scripts on every page, while also keeping `activeTab`, `tabs`, `scripting`, and `alarms`. `scripting` is not used in the reviewed sources, `activeTab` is largely redundant with always-on host permissions, and the combination gives the extension a much broader attack surface than the
issues.suggested_fix: Reduce permissions to the minimum needed: drop unused `scripting`, reconsider `activeTab` vs global host access, and prefer narrower host patterns or on-demand injection instead of unconditional `<all_urls>` content scripts.

issues.description: The service worker installs a recurring `chrome.alarms` poller at parse time and on startup/install, then hits `http://localhost:8088/dev/version` every 30 seconds forever. That is a dev hot-reload mechanism running in production code, and it needlessly wakes the MV3 worker and depends on a localhost daemon even when the user is not using the extension's development loop.
issues.suggested_fix: Gate the hot-reload alarm behind an explicit development build flag, and keep production service-worker wakeups event-driven instead of using a permanent localhost polling loop.

strengths: On the normal alt-click/auto-label path, `buildAnatomyV1()` itself is aligned with the current draft-07 schema and Zod validator: it emits `version`, `metadata`, `regions`, `chains`, and `layers` with
The background message handlers correctly keep MV3 async channels open by returning `true` for the asynchronous branches, so the basic capture/fetch/ping/post reply pattern is sound.
The serializer helpers in `lib/` are reasonably isolated and already have focused `_smoke*.mjs` coverage for adopted stylesheets, runtime inline styles, shadow DOM, and canvas pre-capture.
I did not find `eval`, `new Function`, or similar code-generation primitives in the reviewed extension sources.
